content: I'm implementing an operation where I'm looping over a collection, and I could periodically release and retake the lock if another thread is waiting for it. Does the kernel have any way to check whether anybody else is trying to take the lock?

Wouldn't you get that effect if you just release the lock and try to reacquire it immediately? IIUC other threads waiting for it would acquire it before you can re-acquire it, providing the desired behavior. And if nobody is waiting, then your thread continues as if nothing happened
yeah but if I release it I have to traverse the red/black tree to find my location again
is it a spinlock? If so there is spin_is_contended
Maybe another reason to deabstractify the locks
I guess spin_is_contended might apply, although it's rather obscure and doesn't have many users so you will want to double-check before relying on it

worst case, you can maybe define your own custom spinlock-derived type that includes a counter which is increased whenever a thread tries to acquire the lock. That way to thread currently holding the lock can check the counter to know whether it should release it.
(but I bet you already thought about that :))

What in the world does this mean?

#define spin_is_contended(lock)         (((void)(lock), 0))

Is it just a fancy way to say "always false"?
yes, apparently this depends on architecture support so might not be available everywhere :grimacing:
Do you have a reason to believe this is an optimization?
